overview: Adds opt-in, per-client rate limiting to the HTTP API via a small token-bucket middleware. The bucket is configured from env, keyed per API client, and fails open if the limiter store is unavailable. Routes opt in by mounting the middleware; everything else is unchanged.

steps.title: 1 · Config is opt-in
steps.body: Limits come from env (RATE_LIMIT_RPS, RATE_LIMIT_BURST) with safe defaults. When RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED is unset the middleware is a no-op, so existing deployments behave exactly as before.

steps.title: 2 · The token-bucket core
steps.body: Each client gets a bucket that refills at rps tokens/second up to burst. A request costs one token; an empty bucket returns 429 with a Retry-After header derived from the refill rate.
suggestions: How is the refill computed between requests?

steps.title: 3 · Per-client keying
steps.body: The bucket key is the authenticated client id, falling back to the peer IP for unauthenticated routes. Keys are namespaced so two routes can't collide in the shared store.
suggestions: Could two clients share a key?

steps.title: 4 · Fail open, not closed
steps.body: If the store throws (e.g. the cache is down), the middleware logs and calls next() rather than rejecting traffic — availability is preferred over strict enforcement for this tier.

steps.title: 5 · What's covered
steps.body: Tests assert: tokens deplete and refill over time, a depleted bucket returns 429 with Retry-After, distinct clients are isolated, and a throwing store falls open.
suggestions: Is the refill timing tested deterministically?
